title: What is a GRU?
concept: Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs) are a popular, simplified variation of LSTMs that merge the cell state and hidden state, running faster and using less memory.

exercises.prompt: GRUs are a simplified and faster cousin of which neural network type?
exercises.explain: GRUs are designed for sequential data (like text or audio) and are a faster, simplified version of LSTMs.

cards.title: Streamlining Memory
cards.body: LSTMs are great at remembering long sequences, but they can be slow and memory-heavy. What if we could cut out the clutter and make a neural network that is just as smart but runs much faster? Enter the GRU!

cards.title: What is a GRU?
cards.body: A Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) is a streamlined type of Recurrent Neural Network. Unlike LSTMs which keep track of two separate memory streams, a GRU merges them into one single hidden state. It uses update and reset gates to decide what information to keep or throw away.

cards.title: The Hybrid Backpack
cards.body: Think of an LSTM like a traveler carrying both a heavy suitcase (cell state) and a backpack (hidden state). A GRU is like a traveler who combined them into one smart hybrid backpack. They carry less weight and move through the airport much faster!

cards.title: GRUs in Action
cards.body: Imagine building a voice assistant for a smartwatch. Because the watch has a small battery and limited processing power, a standard LSTM might be too slow. By using a GRU instead, the assistant processes your spoken commands quickly and uses much less battery power.

cards.title: Is More Complex Always Better?
cards.body: Many think that because LSTMs are more complex, they are always better. In reality, GRUs often perform just as well as LSTMs on many tasks, while training faster and requiring less computational power.

cards.title: LSTM vs. GRU Structure
cards.body: Let's look at how the memory structure compares between these two sequential models.

left.text: Two states: Cell State + Hidden State (Uses 3 gates)

right.text: One merged state: Hidden State only (Uses 2 gates)

exercises.prompt: How do GRUs simplify the memory structure compared to LSTMs?

options.text: They add a third memory stream
They merge the cell state and hidden state into one
They only work on image data

exercises.explain: While LSTMs maintain both a cell state and a hidden state, GRUs simplify this by merging them into one single hidden state.

exercises.prompt: True or False: GRUs generally train faster and use less memory than LSTMs.
exercises.explain: Because GRUs have fewer parameters and gates, they require less math per step, making them faster to train than LSTMs.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing gate name that controls how much old information to keep.
exercises.sentence: The [blank] gate in a GRU decides how much of the past information to keep.
exercises.explain: The update gate in a GRU decides how much of the past memory to pass along to the future, acting like a filter.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes a GRU?

options.text: A model that uses three separate gates to manage a distinct cell state and hidden state.
A streamlined recurrent network that merges states and uses update and reset gates.
A simple feedforward network that cannot process sequential data at all.

exercises.explain: The second description refers to a standard LSTM, which has three gates (input, forget, output) instead of the GRU's two gates (update, reset).

exercises.prompt: Why would a machine learning engineer choose a GRU over a standard LSTM?

options.text: They have more gates and parameters than LSTMs
They do not support sequential data processing
They merge states to run faster with fewer parameters
They completely eliminate the use of activation functions

exercises.explain: GRUs are popular because they offer a fantastic balance: they solve the vanishing gradient problem like LSTMs, but they do it faster and with fewer parameters because they merge the cell and hidden states.
